chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
Why is TLV not considered a data representation format?
It appears in many packet formats and could be used to encode data with schema.

chat.author: Esko Dijk
chat.text: Besides TLV, there's also LTV (actually used in a spec I know)  :)

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: And there is TLV.

chat.author: Esko Dijk
chat.text: During the hackathon I discovered that "JSON with // comments" also exists.  (Not valid JSON.)

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
"JSON with // comments" are known as either "jsonc" and "json5".
"jsonc" format is used in Visual Studio Code configuration files, allowing only "//" and "/* */" comments.
"json5" format has more extensions such as allowing extra comma at end of array/map.
These are more like coding languages and less like data representation formats.
I don't accept these formats when I design network endpoints and instead tell the client developer to convert back to strict JSON.

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: So no inline documentation...

chat.author: Emil Lundberg
chat.text: If you want inline documentation, then that sounds like you want a configuration language like TOML rather than a data interchange format :)

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: BTW, when I did CODACYL schemas (circa '83), we did LOTS of comments.

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
In my recent coding projects, the configuration file is ECMAScript module that exports a JavaScript object.
This allows comments and Turing-complete generation methods.
The exported JavaScript object must conform to a JSON Schema or TypeScript type.

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: And those were configurations, not interchange stuff.

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
I've seen other project generating JSON objects like:
{
  "_comment0": "..",
  "_comment1": "..",
  "_comment2": "..",
  "actual_key": "actual_value"
}

chat.author: Mohamed Boucadair
chat.text: that approach we used in RFCs such as RFC9834 for comments in JSON

chat.author: Esko Dijk
chat.text: So to try this: go to https://cbor.me , and paste in some JSON object in the left column and click the green arrow.

chat.author: Ken Takayama
chat.text: Comments are supported in CBOR Extended Diagnostic Notation, as defined in draft-ietf-cbor-edn-literals, but cannot be represented in the actual CBOR binary encoding.

chat.author: Julius Rüberg
chat.text: Is there a non self-describing standard like protobuf based on cddl?

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
In Named Data Networking format, we had a recent incident due to the lack of "Conditionally Present" indication in ABNF.
SigInfo = SigType [KeyLocator]
SigType = SIG-TYPE-TYPE TLV-LENGTH NonNegativeInteger

If SigType value is one of {1,3,4,5}, KeyLocator is mandatory.
If SigType value is one of {0,6}, KeyLocator is forbidden.

Many people read the ABNF and think KeyLocator is "optional", as we don't have a better way to write it in ABNF.
Does CDDL have a "Conditionally Present" indication so that we can write the spec more precisely?

chat.author: David Slik
chat.text: Are there provisions for 4 byte alignment of binary data?

chat.author: Brian Sipos
chat.text: Embedded CBOR is very powerful for isolating layers: A CBOR wrapper from Protocol A can contain CBOR data from Protocol B, and the processing of the two (encode or decode) are isolated and can even be done e.g. by two different libraries.

chat.author: Ira McDonald
chat.text: Carsten - please stay on the mike - you're fading...

chat.author: Brian Sipos
chat.text: Embedded CBOR is also valuable for creating skippable subtrees

chat.author: Emil Lundberg
chat.text:
Junxiao Shi said:

In Named Data Networking format, we had a recent incident due to the lack of "Conditionally Present" indication in ABNF.
SigInfo = SigType [KeyLocator]
SigType = SIG-TYPE-TYPE TLV-LENGTH NonNegativeInteger

If SigType value is one of {1,3,4,5}, KeyLocator is mandatory.
If SigType value is one of {0,6}, KeyLocator is forbidden.

Many people read the ABNF and think KeyLocator is "optional", as we don't have a better way to write it in ABNF.
Does CDDL have a "Conditionally Present" indication so that we can write the spec more precisely?

If I understand you correctly, I think you can do that with CDDL groups:
SigType = (1 / 3 / 4 / 5, KeyLocator // 0 / 6)

chat.author: Brian Sipos
chat.text: If the patterns are prefix-based and mutually exclusive it is also possible to write CDDL as (possibly many) alternatives.

chat.author: Emil Lundberg
chat.text:
...(cont'd) or even more explicitly using the .and operator:
SigType = ( SigType .and (1 / 3 / 4 / 5), KeyLocator
//
SigType .and (0 / 6) )
